name: Harae (Purification)
shortDescription: Harae is a Shinto purification ritual performed to cleanse individuals, objects, or locations of impurity (kegare) and restore harmony.
description: Harae (祓, 祓へ) is a fundamental concept in Shinto, the indigenous religion of Japan, referring to the act of purification. It encompasses a wide range of rituals and practices designed to remove impurity, defilement, or misfortune (kegare) and restore a state of purity and harmony. This purification can be applied to individuals, objects, or even entire locations. The concept is deeply rooted in Shinto cosmology, which emphasizes the importance of maintaining a balance between the sacred and the profane. Kegare can arise from various sources, including contact with death, illness, or wrongdoing, and it is believed to disrupt the flow of spiritual energy (ki) and create disharmony. Harae aims to rectify this imbalance and restore the individual or object to a state of spiritual cleanliness, allowing them to interact positively with the kami (spirits or deities). Different forms of harae exist, ranging from simple personal purification practices like rinsing the mouth and hands at a shrine's temizuya (water basin) to elaborate ceremonies performed by priests. Large-scale harae might involve the use of salt, water, paper streamers (gohei), and incantations (norito). The story of Izanagi's purification after his descent to Yomi (the land of the dead) is a foundational myth illustrating the importance of harae in Shinto. The purification process allowed Izanagi to cleanse himself of the defilement of the underworld and subsequently give birth to the major kami of Japan. The placement of shimenawa ropes at sacred sites and torii gates at shrine entrances are also physical manifestations of the desire to separate the sacred from the profane and maintain a purified space. The sakaki tree, considered sacred, is also used in harae rituals.

sources.title: Living Kami: Toward a Practical Theology of Shinto
sources.author: Daniel A. Métraux
